era_context: The Western Roman Empire is in acute military and political crisis. Stilicho continues campaigns against Alaric and other barbarian groups. The Eastern Roman Empire under Arcadius faces Persian pressure and internal factional conflict. In China, the Eastern Jin Dynasty is fragmenting. Christianity is now institutionally dominant across the Mediterranean world. This is a period of rapid transformation in political structures, military organisation, and religious authority across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia and the Indian subcontinent

events.title: Stilicho's military operations in the Balkans and Greece
events.description: Stilicho continues his military campaigns to contain Alaric's Visigothic forces and other barbarian groups in the Balkans and Greece. These operations are part of a sustained effort to maintain Roman military control over the eastern Balkans and prevent barbarian incursion into Italy. The campaigns are partially successful tactically but fail to resolve the underlying political crisis of barbarian integration and Roman military fragmentation.
events.certainty_note: Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence, but the exact chronology of individual campaigns is uncertain. The year 398 CE is a conventional date for Stilicho's operations in this period.

events.title: John Chrysostom becomes Patriarch of Constantinople
events.description: John Chrysostom (c. 347–407 CE), a renowned Christian theologian and orator, is appointed Patriarch of Constantinople, the highest ecclesiastical office in the Eastern Roman Empire. His appointment marks a significant moment in the consolidation of Christian institutional power and the integration of the Church into imperial governance. Chrysostom's tenure is marked by his efforts to reform clerical discipline and his conflicts with the imperial court, particularly with the Empress Eudoxia. His appointment reflects the Church's growing political influence in late antique society.
key_figures: John Chrysostom (Patriarch of Constantinople, c. 347–407 CE)

sources.name: Palladius, Dialogue on the Life of John Chrysostom (early 5th century)
John Chrysostom's own writings and homilies (primary texts)

events.certainty_note: John Chrysostom's appointment as Patriarch in 398 CE is well-documented in multiple sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Eastern Jin Dynasty: Liu Yu gains military prominence
events.description: Liu Yu (劉裕, 363–422 CE), a military commander of humble origins, begins to gain prominence in the Eastern Jin court. Over the next two decades, Liu Yu will consolidate military power and eventually found the Liu Song Dynasty in 420 CE. His rise exemplifies the pattern of military strongmen superseding aristocratic authority in late Eastern Jin politics. Liu Yu's career marks the transition from the Eastern Jin to the Northern and Southern Dynasties period.
key_figures: Liu Yu (future founder of Liu Song Dynasty, 363–422 CE)
Emperor Longxu of Eastern Jin (r. 397–402 CE)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Liu Yu's rise to prominence is well-documented in Chinese historiography, though the specific date of 398 CE for his early prominence is conventional.
cross_references: 420-001 (Founding of Liu Song Dynasty by Liu Yu)

events.title: Sassanid Persia under Yazdegerd I: consolidation of power
events.description: Yazdegerd I (r. 399–421 CE, though his reign may have begun slightly earlier) consolidates power in the Sassanid Persian Empire. His reign is marked by administrative reforms, religious tolerance (including toward Christians and Zoroastrians), and military campaigns against the Eastern Roman Empire and Central Asian nomads. Yazdegerd I is one of the most significant Sassanid rulers and represents a period of Persian imperial strength during the late antique period.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (9th century compilation)

events.certainty_note: The date of Yazdegerd I's accession is debated; some sources place it in 399 CE, others in 401 CE. The Pahlavi sources are contemporary but fragmentary. Byzantine sources are later and biased.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional dating of John Chrysostom's appointment as Patriarch to 398 CE is secure, but the significance attributed to his appointment in narratives of 'Church triumphalism' has been challenged by modern scholarship, which emphasises the conflicts and limitations of ecclesiastical power in the late antique period. Chrysostom's conflicts with the imperial court and his eventual exile (407 CE) demonstrate the fragility of clerical authority. The dating of Yazdegerd I's accession is disputed; some sources place it as late as 401 CE. Liu Yu's early prominence in 398 CE is a conventional date; the sources do not precisely date his rise.
historiographic_note: The year 398 CE is moderately documented in surviving sources, but most information comes from later compilations. Claudian's poems provide near-contemporary evidence for the Western Empire but are propagandistic. Ecclesiastical sources (Socrates Scholasticus, Palladius) provide information on John Chrysostom but are biased toward religious and moral dimensions. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled centuries later. Sassanid sources are fragmentary and often preserved only in later Islamic compilations. The year 398 CE is a conventional marker in modern historiography; the actual significance of events in this specific year is difficult to assess with precision.

graph_edges.note: Stilicho's operations in 398 CE continued into the campaigns of 399 CE
John Chrysostom's appointment as Patriarch in 398 CE led to his conflicts with the imperial court and eventual exile in 407 CE
Liu Yu's rise to prominence in 398 CE culminated in his founding of Liu Song Dynasty in 420 CE
Yazdegerd I's consolidation of power in 398 CE was part of his broader reign from 399–421 CE
